title: The Premiere

jokes.text: As your publicist, it is my job to optimize a branded approach to real-time engagement with your fanbase through contextual marketing. I hope I'm not paying you by the word.
jokes.setup: Alexis delivers an overly verbose, corporate-speak explanation of her publicist duties
jokes.punchline: Moira's dry response about paying by the word deflates the pretentious language
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Alexis trying to sound professional with buzzword salad, and Moira's sharp wit cutting through it. Economic and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm essentially a multi-platform digital strategist who leverages synergistic brand optimization through targeted stakeholder engagement and strategic narrative dissemination across diversified mediums.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Do they pay you by the word?

jokes.text: Which is why I came up with the ideation for the following idea.
jokes.setup: Alexis continues her corporate speak
jokes.punchline: The redundancy of 'ideation for the following idea' reveals her shallow understanding of business jargon
jokes.explanation: Continues the character beat of Alexis misusing business language, though less impactful than the first joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We need to really focus on ideation for the following idea.

jokes.text: Alexis, your flip-chart says 'premiere.' / What're you trying to say? / I'm sorry, why are you even involved in this conversation?
jokes.setup: David points out inconsistency in Alexis's presentation
jokes.punchline: Alexis deflects by questioning David's right to be involved rather than addressing the contradiction
jokes.explanation: Classic sibling dynamic with Alexis's defensive deflection being perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, you just said you were going to focus on sustainable sourcing, but then you also said you didn't care about where the products came from.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, well, why are you even involved in this? This is my thing.

jokes.text: Oh, sure, for a scrappy little novice, but not for someone who's cut many a crimson rug.
jokes.setup: Moira responds to the idea of a local premiere
jokes.punchline: Her pretentious phrasing 'cut many a crimson rug' for 'walked red carpets' is classic Moira vocabulary
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Moira - taking a common phrase and making it unnecessarily ornate. Great character integration and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A local premiere? Well, I have certainly cut many a crimson rug in my day.

jokes.text: What's next, I sign my name in the pavement outside Bob's Garage? Jocelyn gives you an honourary degree at her school? Host the Met Ga-la at David's store?
jokes.setup: Moira lists increasingly absurd scenarios of being 'honored' in inappropriate venues
jokes.punchline: The escalation from pavement signing to hosting the Met Gala at David's store shows her dramatic flair
jokes.explanation: Well-structured escalation that perfectly captures Moira's grandiose perspective and horror at small-town limitations.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I could be honored at the opening of a new parking garage. They could name a parking space after me. They could paint my face on the pavement.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Or perhaps they'll host a gala in my honor. A grand, elegant affair.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Actually, they could host the Met Gala at David's store.

jokes.text: I don't like that one.
jokes.setup: Moira suggests hosting the Met Gala at David's store
jokes.punchline: David's understated objection to the most absurd suggestion
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - just four words that perfectly capture David's deadpan reaction to his mother's ridiculous suggestion.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The Met Gala? At my store? Mother, my store is 800 square feet. The Met Gala is in New York. And I don't have the infrastructure, the clientele, or frankly, the will to host that event.

jokes.text: Roland has a guy who rents out live crows.
jokes.setup: Alexis details her premiere plans
jokes.punchline: The absurd specificity of Roland having a crow rental contact
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd detail that both sets up the episode's climax and perfectly captures the show's small-town surrealism.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm having a premiere party for my movie, and I need everything to be absolutely perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you know, I happen to have a contact who rents crows.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why would you have a contact who rents crows?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You'd be surprised how often that comes up.

jokes.text: Remember that 'Waterworld II' premiere? Exactly, David. Oh, the last place anyone wanted to be when those reviews came in was a glass-bottomed pirate ship in the middle of the ocean.
jokes.setup: Moira recalls a previous premiere disaster
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific and over-the-top venue of a glass-bottomed pirate ship for a Waterworld sequel premiere
jokes.explanation: Fantastic world-building joke that establishes Moira's Hollywood past with an absurdly specific and visual detail.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I once attended a premiere on a glass-bottomed pirate ship for a Waterworld sequel.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A glass-bottomed pirate ship? For a Waterworld sequel?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, it was very on-brand for the studio at the time.

jokes.text: Good luck though. / Lick rust! Thank you!
jokes.setup: David wishes Moira good luck sarcastically
jokes.punchline: Moira's mishearing creates an absurd response that she delivers as if it were perfectly normal
jokes.explanation: Brilliant mishearing joke that creates a nonsensical but confident response. Economic and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Good luck.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Thank you, I'm going to need all the luck I can get.

jokes.text: I do. It's a death trap that needs to be set on fire and pushed off a cliff.
jokes.setup: Patrick says he doesn't know what's wrong with his car
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deadpan but extreme assessment of the car's condition
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie delivery - matter-of-fact tone delivering an extreme judgment. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I don't know what's wrong with my car.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's dead. It's completely dead. I would need to examine it more closely, but I'm fairly certain the car is deceased.

jokes.text: driving his fiancé to go get his wisdom teeth out is totally within my maid-of honour responsibilities.
jokes.setup: Stevie explains why she's driving Patrick
jokes.punchline: Her sarcastic interpretation of maid-of-honor duties being stretched to include dental appointments
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Stevie's dry humor about being roped into wedding duties.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Apparently being maid of honor now includes dental appointments. I didn't realize that was in the job description, but here we are.

jokes.text: Also, if this is what it takes to get you out of that dank little apartment...
jokes.setup: Patrick is trying to be supportive
jokes.punchline: His backhanded comment about Stevie's living situation
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe humor from Patrick's well-meaning but tactless comment.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's great that you're embracing the whole... rustic charm thing. Not everyone can pull off living in a motel room. Most people would find it depressing, but you seem to have a real knack for making the best of it.

jokes.text: Look at me. Two years ago, I thought I'd have a wife and kid at this point. / Yeah, well, one out of two isn't bad. / I've been told I'm... very young at heart.
jokes.setup: Patrick reflects on his life plans not working out
jokes.punchline: Stevie's misdirection suggesting he's the 'kid' rather than having one, followed by his earnest response
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection joke with perfect character work - Stevie's wit and Patrick's oblivious earnestness.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I just thought by now I'd have a house, a car, maybe even a kid.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're not having a kid?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, eventually, maybe. Why?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know, you just seem like you could use one.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's... I'm not sure how to take that.

jokes.text: Hello, Access Hollywood? Have I got a star sighting for you! Very pawky, Jocelyn.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn makes an over-the-top announcement about Moira's arrival
jokes.punchline: Moira's use of 'pawky' (Scottish for slyly humorous) as a put-down
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira vocabulary moment, using an obscure word as a sophisticated insult.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: And now, the woman we've all been waiting for... Moira Rose!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's very kind of you to say, though I wouldn't call it a 'pawky' introduction.

jokes.text: save yourselves the trip to Frocks4Less. From what I've heard, it's more of a minor league viewing party.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is excited about buying a dress
jokes.punchline: Moira's dismissive comments about both the store name and event status
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Moira's condescension toward small-town commerce and events.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A dress from 'The Dress Barn'? Jocelyn, that's not a store, that's a zoning violation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And it's not a gala, it's a county fair ribbon-cutting ceremony.

jokes.text: It reminds me of that time that Renée Zellweger's brother came in here, and I got him to sign a menu. I mean, he spelled his name differently, but I swear it was him.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn shares her excitement about celebrity encounters
jokes.punchline: The logical contradiction of getting an autograph from someone who spelled their name differently than the celebrity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jocelyn moment - her naive excitement overriding obvious logical flaws in her celebrity encounter.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh my gosh, I got an autograph from a celebrity!

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: But wait, they spelled their name completely differently than how it's actually spelled.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: So now I have an autograph from someone who isn't actually them.

jokes.text: Ornithology Today! Well, if anyone knows crows...
jokes.setup: The review is from a bird science publication
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's matter-of-fact acceptance that bird scientists are the ultimate movie critics
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the absurdity of treating an ornithology journal as a legitimate movie review source.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, of course bird scientists would be the ultimate movie critics. They have the best perspective on everything.

jokes.text: Well, that's hyperbolic. I mean, they already had 'fun' and now they've added 'good.'
jokes.setup: The review calls the movie 'good fun'
jokes.punchline: Moira's overthinking of the phrase as if 'good' and 'fun' are separate escalating compliments
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira over-analysis of simple language, treating casual praise as dramatic escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Good fun. So it's good, but not great. Fun, but not hilarious.

jokes.text: We were resolute about not making an issues movie, but I'm glad they picked up on that.
jokes.setup: The review mentions emotional depth regarding crows
jokes.punchline: Moira's contradiction - claiming they avoided making an issues movie while being glad the review found social commentary
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Moira's contradictory thinking about her artistic choices.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We were very careful not to make an issues movie.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So I'm thrilled the reviewer found such rich social commentary about the plight of crows.

jokes.text: Moira Rose, as Dr. Clara Mandrake, is delightfully unhinged. / Do you know what they call this, gals? An understated rave.
jokes.setup: The review calls Moira 'delightfully unhinged'
jokes.punchline: Moira interpreting this backhanded compliment as high praise
jokes.explanation: Perfect character beat - Moira's ability to reframe any criticism as praise, plus her theatrical language.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Delightfully unhinged. That's exactly what I was going for.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Moira, that was a backhanded compliment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Was it? Because I'm choosing to hear it as high praise. Delightfully unhinged. It's rather fabulous, actually.

jokes.text: Well, you might want to tell your friend Janet that there just may be a bombshell attendee after all.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn mentions her journalist friend
jokes.punchline: Moira referring to herself as a 'bombshell attendee' with complete seriousness
jokes.explanation: Good Moira self-aggrandizement moment, perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: My journalist friend is coming to the event.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose I'll have to make myself available as a bombshell attendee.

jokes.text: Oh, but do pack eyedrops! The film's first third is densely packed with exposition, and you won't want to blink.
jokes.setup: Moira gives viewing advice
jokes.punchline: Her unintentionally honest admission that the movie is boring exposition disguised as helpful advice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment where Moira inadvertently reveals the movie's flaws while trying to be helpful.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: If you're going to watch this, I suggest you do so with the sound off and subtitles on. That way you can at least pretend you're reading something intellectually stimulating rather than watching a two-hour advertisement for a lifestyle you'll never have.

jokes.text: Oh, it's actually best. A lot of this film blatantly contradicts what's been established in the first two.
jokes.setup: Someone asks if it's okay they haven't seen the first two movies
jokes.punchline: Moira's casual admission that the film ignores its own continuity
jokes.explanation: Another great moment of Moira inadvertently revealing the film's poor quality while maintaining her dignity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, it's perfectly fine. The film completely ignores its own continuity anyway.

jokes.text: Thank you so much for all the hot tips you were giving our Uber driver. For a second, I was concerned about my rating, but I think he seemed to enjoy all the wrong directions you were yelling at him.
jokes.setup: David sarcastically thanks Patrick
jokes.punchline: The image of Patrick giving wrong directions while high on anesthetic
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Patrick's drugged state, with David's characteristic sarcasm.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you, Patrick. Thank you for that. Because what I really needed today was to get completely lost following directions from someone who's still half-unconscious from dental anesthesia.

jokes.text: ♪ I'm a hungry, hungry hippo
jokes.setup: Patrick says he's hungry
jokes.punchline: He breaks into the children's game song while drugged
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective physical comedy showing Patrick's altered state.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm hungry.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: The wheels on the bus go round and round, round and round, round and round...

jokes.text: Why are you so handsome? / How come your skin is like... perfect. / It's a nine-step regimen I do twice a day. It's not a big deal.
jokes.setup: Drugged Patrick compliments David's appearance
jokes.punchline: David's matter-of-fact response about his skincare routine despite claiming it's 'not a big deal'
jokes.explanation: Perfect David moment - his vanity showing through even when trying to downplay his elaborate routine.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You have the most beautiful skin I've ever seen.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I do have a very rigorous skincare routine, but it's not a big deal.

jokes.text: You know what I really wanna do with you? / Uh-huh. Ethically speaking, I don't think we can do that with you in this state. / Have a baby.
jokes.setup: Patrick's suggestive lead-up makes David assume something sexual
jokes.punchline: The innocent revelation that Patrick wants to have a baby
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect character work - David's assumption and Patrick's innocent intent.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, there's something I've been wanting to talk to you about. Something I think could really change our lives.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Patrick, not here.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I think we should do it soon. I've been thinking about it constantly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick!

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I want to have a baby.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Like I wanna have like a little baby with you. And we could just love it and hold it and bounce it. / Yeah, that's a... that's a big one-eighty.
jokes.setup: Patrick elaborates on wanting a baby
jokes.punchline: David's understated reaction to this major emotional revelation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing David's discomfort with emotional vulnerability, even from his drugged fiancé.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I've been thinking about it for a while now. I think I want to have a baby.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: You would be such a good dad. / Have we met? / I wish you were my dad.
jokes.setup: Patrick says David would be a good father
jokes.punchline: The progression from David's self-deprecation to Patrick's bizarre wish
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from David's realistic self-assessment to Patrick's druggy non-sequitur about family dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You'd be a great father.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I can barely take care of myself. I once forgot to water my plants for three months and cried when they died.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That just means you have a capacity for love.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It means I'm negligent and emotionally unstable.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I wish you'd have my baby.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not how biology works, and also, no.

jokes.text: I'm glad you didn't feel you needed to clean up on my account, you know? It shows just how comfortable a friendship we actually have. / I did clean up. / Right. That's... what I meant.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to compliment Stevie's casual approach
jokes.punchline: The revelation that she actually did clean up, followed by Johnny's awkward backtrack
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment with Johnny's well-meaning comment backfiring spectacularly, followed by his terrible recovery attempt.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, Stevie, I really admire how you have such a carefree attitude about... tidiness.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Actually, I spent three hours cleaning this place.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, then you have a very clean carefree attitude about tidiness.

jokes.text: Good. We bought the second motel! / First phase of many in the Rosebud Motel expansion.
jokes.setup: Johnny updates Stevie on business developments
jokes.punchline: His grandiose language about 'expansion phases' for what's just buying a second small motel
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Johnny's tendency to inflate modest business achievements.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie, we're entering an exciting new phase. We're talking expansion, we're talking empire building, we're talking about acquiring a second property.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You bought another motel?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're not just 'buying' anything, Stevie. We're strategically positioning ourselves in the marketplace. This is about portfolio diversification, about leveraging our brand equity across multiple revenue streams.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's a motel two towns over, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's a *gateway* motel, Stevie. It's the beginning of something extraordinary.

jokes.text: But not on your bed.
jokes.setup: Johnny wants to sit down to talk business
jokes.punchline: His awkward specification about not sitting on her bed
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe humor from Johnny's unnecessary specification, implying awareness of the awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We need to sit down and talk business.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But not on your bed. I don't want to sit on your bed.

jokes.text: I wanna buy you out. / Oh!
jokes.setup: Johnny builds up to his 'proposal'
jokes.punchline: The unexpected revelation that he wants to buy her out, not bring her back
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that subverts both Stevie's and the audience's expectations about Johnny's intentions.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie, I've been thinking about us, about the business, about our future together.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And I've realized that what I really want is to buy you out of the Rosebud Motel.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You... you want to buy me out?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes. I think it's the best move for both of us.

jokes.text: You don't have to downplay your excitement on my account, Stevie. I'll be just fine.
jokes.setup: Stevie shows no visible excitement about the book
jokes.punchline: Johnny's comment implies she's trying to hide enthusiasm when she's clearly unimpressed
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from Johnny's misreading of Stevie's reaction, typical character obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, Stevie, your poker face is slipping. I can tell you're absolutely thrilled about this book.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm not thrilled.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you're doing a wonderful job of hiding it.

jokes.text: Oh, it's barely noticeable, dear. We'll find you an electrologist.
jokes.setup: Alexis says she has a problem
jokes.punchline: Moira assumes it's a physical appearance issue and offers a beauty treatment solution
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Moira's priorities and assumptions about problems.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I have a problem.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh sweetie, is it your skin? Because I have a dermatologist who can work miracles. Or your hair? I know someone in Toronto who specializes in damage control. We could do a weekend trip.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, Mom, it's not that.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, then it must be your nails. Or possibly your entire aura.

jokes.text: When Demi decided on a Friday night at 6pm that she wanted to celebrate her 40th in Aspen, her publicist had us in parkas by 7. Sadly, Megan went into cardiac arrest on the flight, but she perished doing what she loved.
jokes.setup: Moira tells a story about last-minute party planning
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of someone dying on a celebrity birthday flight
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy delivered with Moira's characteristic casual tone about Hollywood excess and tragedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I was planning a birthday soirée for a very prominent socialite, and naturally, everything had to be absolutely flawless. I had secured a private jet to transport the guests, but wouldn't you know it, someone died on the flight.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But did that stop me? No. I simply had the body removed at the next stop, refreshed my lipstick, and we were back on schedule by appetizers.

jokes.text: That was mine. I've been looking for that. / Blame UPS.
jokes.setup: Alexis realizes Moira is wearing her dress
jokes.punchline: Moira's casual deflection of blame to the shipping company
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Moira's ability to deflect responsibility with absurd casualness.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's my dress!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, the shipping company labeled it so poorly, how was I supposed to know?

jokes.text: with the baby around, I only get that kind of me-time maybe... five to six nights a week, so...
jokes.setup: Ronnie complains about lack of personal time due to baby
jokes.punchline: The revelation that she still gets 5-6 nights a week of 'me time'
jokes.explanation: Good character humor showing Ronnie's self-centered perspective on parenthood.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I haven't had a minute to myself since the baby was born. It's constant. I'm exhausted.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I don't even remember the last time I had any me time.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Well, I guess I get five, six nights a week when Felix takes the baby and I'm alone in my room, but that doesn't count.

jokes.text: 'cause my skinny jeans are in the wash.
jokes.setup: Ronnie worries about being ready for a premiere
jokes.punchline: Her concern about laundry rather than the event itself
jokes.explanation: Simple character moment showing Ronnie's practical priorities over glamour.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I'm not ready for this premiere. I don't have anything clean to wear.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I've got dirty underwear piled up to the ceiling. Do I look like I have time to do laundry before tonight?

jokes.text: Fine. But you owe me a prime spot on that carpet. / Wait, you wanna walk the carpet? / Honey, it's a movie premiere, is it not?
jokes.setup: Ronnie agrees to help with conditions
jokes.punchline: Her expectation of red carpet treatment at a small-town event
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ronnie's self-importance and desire for recognition.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I'll help you out, but I got conditions.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Of course you do. What are they?

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I want the red carpet treatment. Full VIP situation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ronnie, it's the opening of a new Subway in Schitt's Creek.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: And? I still deserve to be treated like royalty.

jokes.text: I would like to request a time before Roland. And I'd like to go first.
jokes.setup: Discussion of red carpet arrival times
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's competitive demands about upstaging Roland and going first
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing small-town rivalry and ego over a tiny premiere.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I'm going first. I don't care what Roland's doing or what time he's showing up. I'm upstaging that man, and I'm going first.

jokes.text: It's, uh, being finicky. / Uh, remember, Mr. Rose? I left you a sticky note.
jokes.setup: Johnny claims technical difficulties
jokes.punchline: Stevie's revelation that she left instructions, undermining his excuse
jokes.explanation: Mild embarrassment humor showing Johnny's technological ineptitude and excuses.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm having some technical difficulties.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You left instructions.

jokes.text: Is it Larry Air? 'Cause they called me for a reference, and also asked if I had any interest in becoming a pilot.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks about Stevie's job decision
jokes.punchline: The absurd revelation that an airline wanted him as a pilot based on a reference call
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor showing Johnny's tangential thinking and the show's surreal small-town elements.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So what did the airline say about your application?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: They want to hire me as a pilot.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A pilot? Stevie, you don't know anything about flying planes.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I know. Apparently they called your reference and you told them I was 'excellent under pressure.'

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was talking about your ability to work the front desk during busy check-ins!

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, they're very excited to have me start next week.

jokes.text: Roland. I know. He gave me his new business card. Never saw so many typos on a business card.
jokes.setup: Johnny mentions Roland as a business partner
jokes.punchline: Stevie's matter-of-fact observation about Roland's unprofessional business card
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective character moment establishing Roland's incompetence through Stevie's deadpan observation.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: His business card is just a napkin with his phone number written in crayon.

jokes.text: You complimented my flawless skin and called me the Jewish Channing Tatum, which all tracks.
jokes.setup: David recounts Patrick's drugged compliments
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact acceptance of being compared to Channing Tatum
jokes.explanation: Perfect David moment - his vanity allowing him to accept any comparison to attractive celebrities.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He told me I looked like Channing Tatum. And I said, 'Thank you.'

jokes.text: I'm not married to Christina Aguilera, am I?
jokes.setup: David discusses picturing things that don't happen
jokes.punchline: His specific example of imagining marriage to Christina Aguilera
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing David's specific pop culture references and dramatic examples.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I picture things that don't happen all the time. Like, I'm married to Christina Aguilera.

jokes.text: I was watching 'Bridget Jones's Baby' right before I went under.
jokes.setup: Patrick explains his baby obsession while drugged
jokes.punchline: The revelation that he was influenced by a rom-com about pregnancy
jokes.explanation: Good payoff that explains Patrick's earlier baby talk with a logical but amusing reason.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I think I've always wanted a baby, you know? It's just been this deep, primal thing inside me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: When did this start?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I watched 'Nine Months' with Hugh Grant in 1995.

jokes.text: Why are you dressed like a limo driver? / Your mother insisted.
jokes.setup: David questions Johnny's formal attire
jokes.punchline: Johnny's explanation that Moira demanded the formal dress code
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag about formal wear inappropriate for the venue, with good character dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why are you dressed like that?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Your mother insisted. Apparently, we're having a 'formal dinner.'

jokes.text: Why are your pupils so dilated. / No, yours are. / No, yours are.
jokes.setup: Johnny notices David's dilated pupils
jokes.punchline: Their childish back-and-forth response
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy showing both characters are likely on Patrick's painkillers.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, your pupils are absolutely enormous.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, maybe I'm just happy to see you.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Don't be cute with me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not being cute. I'm being realistic.

jokes.text: Let the crowening commence!
jokes.setup: Moira announces the movie screening
jokes.punchline: Her dramatic pronunciation of the movie's subtitle
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the disaster to come, with Moira's theatrical delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We're having a screening of 'The Crows Have Eyes 3: The Crowening.' It's the third installment in the franchise.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The Crow... en... ing.

jokes.text: 'The Crows Have Eyes III: The Crowening!'
jokes.setup: Moira announcing the movie title
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous subtitle 'The Crowening' (play on 'The Awakening')
jokes.explanation: Excellent pun title that perfectly captures the B-movie horror sequel absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The title is 'The Awakening: The Crowening'.

jokes.text: I know a bit of their language. CAW! CAW-CAW!
jokes.setup: Chaos as crows attack the audience
jokes.punchline: Moira's attempt to communicate with the birds through cawing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Moira maintaining her pretentious confidence even during a bird attack, trying to use 'crow language.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Caw! Caw! No, no, no—CAW! I'm trying to tell you I'm one of you!

jokes.text: This is fucked.
jokes.setup: Complete chaos at the premiere
jokes.punchline: Stevie's matter-of-fact assessment of the situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie moment - her deadpan delivery cutting through the chaos with simple profanity.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, this is a disaster.

jokes.text: Trained! Some of them were painted seagulls.
jokes.setup: Alexis defends her crow choice
jokes.punchline: The revelation that some 'crows' were actually painted seagulls
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist detail that explains the disaster while making it even more ridiculous. Great payoff to the earlier crow setup.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Look, I thought they were crows, okay? They looked like crows!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, those are seagulls. Painted seagulls.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, how was I supposed to know that? They're all black and angry-looking!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The craftsmanship is actually quite impressive. Very architectural.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so I made one tiny mistake with some birds. It's not like I hurt anyone.

jokes.text: It sorta looks like people are running away from your movie, Moira.
jokes.setup: They watch news coverage of the disaster
jokes.punchline: David's observation about the unfortunate optics for the film
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing David's ability to find the worst possible angle in any situation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, the optics on a disaster film actually premiering during a disaster are... not ideal.

jokes.text: It's called an immersive experience, David! / Immersed in lawsuits maybe.
jokes.setup: Alexis defends her event concept
jokes.punchline: David's cynical wordplay about the legal consequences
jokes.explanation: Strong wordplay that perfectly captures David's pessimistic wit turning Alexis's buzzword against her.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's a really solid concept, David. People love events.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, and people also love not being sued. Two great tastes that don't go together.

jokes.text: Tonight, a murder of wild crows attacked would-be audience members who had come to see a movie about a crow attack.
jokes.setup: News report about the premiere disaster
jokes.punchline: The ironic situation of crow attacks at a movie about crow attacks
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor about the ironic situation, though delivered by external character.

dialogue.speaker: News Reporter
dialogue.line: We're following breaking news out of the Schitt's Creek Drive-In where moviegoers attending the premiere of 'The Murder of Crows' were forced to evacuate after an unexpected murder of crows attacked the outdoor venue.

dialogue.speaker: News Reporter
dialogue.line: The irony of crow attacks occurring at a movie premiere about crow attacks is not lost on us.

tmdb_overview: Alexis plans a local premiere for the "Crows" movie, and Moira makes a big splash on the red carpet. David tends to Patrick after his wisdom teeth removal.
insight: Absurdist chaos edges toward elite: 57 jokes in 25 minutes, just shy of greatness.
